title: Database Connection Failure
possible_causes: Database server is not running or has crashed
Incorrect database host, port, or credentials in configuration
Network connectivity issue between service and database pod
Database connection pool exhausted due to connection leaks
Database is overloaded and rejecting new connections
Firewall or network policy rules blocking the database port
recommended_fixes: Verify database pod is running: kubectl get pods -l app=<db-label>
Check service endpoints: kubectl get svc <db-service>
Increase database connection pool size in application config
Verify database credentials in Kubernetes secrets
Check network policies: kubectl get networkpolicies -A
Restart the database: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<db-deployment>

title: DNS Resolution Failure
possible_causes: Kubernetes CoreDNS is not running or has crashed
Service name typo or service does not exist in the namespace
DNS ConfigMap misconfiguration in the cluster
Network policies blocking DNS traffic on UDP port 53
Pod namespace isolation preventing cross-namespace DNS resolution
CoreDNS pod is OOMKilled due to memory pressure
recommended_fixes: Restart CoreDNS: kubectl rollout restart deployment/coredns -n kube-system
Verify service name and namespace: kubectl get svc -A | grep <service-name>
Inspect CoreDNS ConfigMap: kubectl get configmap coredns -n kube-system -o yaml
Increase CoreDNS memory limits if OOMKilled
Verify DNS policy in pod spec: dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
Check network policies blocking port 53: kubectl get networkpolicies -A

title: Port Conflict / Address Already in Use

patterns.match: only one usage of each socket address

possible_causes: Another process or pod is already listening on the same port
Previous application instance was not shut down cleanly
Port defined in service spec conflicts with a system-reserved port
DaemonSet or sidecar container occupying the port
hostPort configuration conflicts with a node-level process
Rapid restart preventing OS from releasing the port socket
recommended_fixes: Identify the conflicting process: kubectl exec <pod> -- ss -tlnp
Delete the conflicting pod: kubectl delete pod <conflicting-pod>
Change the application's port in the Deployment spec
Add a PreStop lifecycle hook to allow graceful socket release
Check for stale NodePort reservations: kubectl get svc -A
Use SO_REUSEPORT socket option if supported by your application

title: CrashLoopBackOff — Container Restart Loop

patterns.match: pod is in a restart loop

possible_causes: Application crashes immediately on startup due to missing dependency
Database or external service unavailable at startup
Missing or invalid environment variables or secrets
Insufficient memory limits causing OOMKilled
Failed liveness probe causing repeated container restarts
Image entrypoint or command exits with non-zero status
recommended_fixes: Check previous container logs: kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Verify all environment variables and secrets are mounted: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Increase memory limits in the pod spec
Fix the underlying root cause (database, DNS, config) triggering the crash
Add initialDelaySeconds to liveness probe to allow startup time
Use kubectl debug to attach to a failing container for inspection

title: Out of Memory — Container Killed by OOM Killer
possible_causes: Memory limit set too low for the application workload
Memory leak in application code causing gradual heap growth
Unexpected traffic spike consuming more memory than provisioned
JVM heap not tuned to match Kubernetes memory limits
Large file processing or caching operations without bounds
Missing memory limits causing noisy-neighbor eviction
recommended_fixes: Increase memory limits in the Deployment spec
Profile memory usage: kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /proc/meminfo
Set JVM heap flags: -Xmx and -Xms matching container limits
Enable memory-based autoscaling with HPA
Add Prometheus memory alerts to catch trends early
Investigate and fix application-level memory leaks

title: Container Image Pull Failure
possible_causes: Container image tag does not exist in the registry
Missing or expired imagePullSecret for private registry
Incorrect image name or registry URL in the Deployment spec
Private registry is unreachable due to network or DNS failure
Registry rate limit exceeded (DockerHub free tier)
Image was deleted from the registry after deployment
recommended_fixes: Verify image tag exists in the registry: docker manifest inspect <image>:<tag>
Create or refresh imagePullSecret: kubectl create secret docker-registry
Link imagePullSecret to service account: kubectl patch sa default -p
Switch to a correct or existing image tag in the Deployment
Configure a registry mirror to avoid DockerHub rate limits
Set imagePullPolicy: Always to ensure fresh image fetches

title: Authentication Failure
possible_causes: Incorrect password or API key stored in Kubernetes secret
Rotated credentials not updated in the secret store
JWT token expired or signed with wrong secret key
Service account permissions revoked or token invalidated
Database user password changed without updating application config
mTLS certificate expired or certificate authority mismatch
recommended_fixes: Update Kubernetes secret with correct credentials
Rotate and redistribute API keys or service account tokens
Verify JWT signing key is consistent across all instances
Inspect secret values: kubectl get secret <secret> -o jsonpath=
Audit RBAC: kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:<ns>:<sa>
Enable secret rotation policies in your secret manager

title: Missing or Invalid Configuration
possible_causes: ConfigMap or Secret not mounted correctly in the pod spec
Environment variable name typo in Deployment spec
Config file path hardcoded to a path that does not exist in the container
New required config key added to code but not added to the deployment
Feature flag or toggle not configured for the environment
Helm values or Kustomize overlay missing a required value
recommended_fixes: Add missing environment variable to the Deployment spec or ConfigMap
Verify ConfigMap is mounted: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Audit all required config keys against the deployment manifest
Use init containers to validate config before app starts
Apply a linting step in CI/CD to catch missing configs before deploy
Update Helm values.yaml with the missing configuration entry

title: Disk / Storage Volume Full
possible_causes: Log files accumulating without rotation on the PersistentVolume
Database WAL or binary log files filling the disk
Container ephemeral storage limit reached
Insufficient PVC size provisioned for the workload
Core dump or heap dump files written without cleanup
Temporary files from batch jobs not cleaned up
recommended_fixes: Clear old log files: kubectl exec <pod> -- find /logs -mtime +7 -delete
Expand PVC size: kubectl patch pvc <pvc> -p '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"50Gi"}}}}'
Enable log rotation with logrotate or structured logging sinks
Add storage usage alerts to Prometheus/Grafana
Migrate to a larger storage class or cloud volume
Implement automatic cleanup jobs for temporary artifacts

title: Permission Denied — Insufficient Privileges
possible_causes: Container running as non-root but accessing root-owned files
PVC mounted with incorrect ownership or permissions
securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem blocking writes
RBAC policy denying the service account the required verb
PodSecurityPolicy or OPA/Gatekeeper blocking the operation
File system mounted with noexec or nosuid flags
verification_steps: kubectl get psp,podsecuritypolicy 2>/dev/null || kubectl get policyexception -A
recommended_fixes: Set correct fsGroup and runAsUser in pod securityContext
Fix PVC permissions: kubectl exec <init-container> -- chown -R <user> <path>
Update RBAC: kubectl create rolebinding <name> --clusterrole=<role>
Remove readOnlyRootFilesystem or mount a writable emptyDir
Add an init container to set correct file ownership before app starts
Review and update PodSecurityPolicy or OPA constraints

title: Application Startup Failure
possible_causes: Missing required dependencies or services unavailable at startup time
Invalid configuration preventing application initialization
External service (database, cache, message broker) not reachable on first connect
Incompatible library versions causing import or linking errors
Missing or corrupted startup files, certificates, or migration scripts
Port binding failure or OS-level resource exhaustion at launch
recommended_fixes: Inspect startup logs: kubectl logs <pod> --previous
Add an init container to wait for all dependencies before app starts
Verify all required environment variables are set in the Deployment
Check that required config files and secrets are correctly mounted
Add startup probes with suitable failureThreshold and periodSeconds
Enable structured logging from the first line to capture init errors early

title: Configuration Validation Failure
possible_causes: ConfigMap or Helm values contain syntactically invalid YAML or JSON
A required config field has a value outside the permitted range or type
Config schema was updated but the ConfigMap was not re-applied
Secrets contain unexpected encoding or trailing whitespace in values
Feature flag configuration references an undefined toggle
Environment-specific override file missing or malformed
recommended_fixes: Validate ConfigMap YAML: kubectl get configmap <name> -o yaml | yamllint -
Fix the offending configuration value identified in the startup logs
Re-apply the corrected ConfigMap: kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml
Add a CI/CD config validation step to catch schema errors before deploy
Use a config management library with strict schema validation at boot
Audit all Secret values for encoding issues: kubectl get secret -o jsonpath

title: Pod Stuck in Pending State

patterns.match: no nodes available to schedule pods
pod has been in pending state
taints that the pod did not tolerate

possible_causes: Cluster nodes lack sufficient CPU or memory to host the pod
Node selectors or affinity rules have no matching nodes
Node taints are not tolerated by the pod spec
PersistentVolumeClaim is not bound (Pending PVC)
ResourceQuota in the namespace is exhausted
Node is cordoned or under maintenance
recommended_fixes: Add more nodes or resize existing ones to provide required capacity
Adjust node selector or affinity rules to match available nodes
Add tolerations to the pod spec for existing node taints
Ensure the PVC is correctly provisioned and bound
Check and increase ResourceQuota limits: kubectl edit resourcequota <name>
Uncordon the target node: kubectl uncordon <node>

title: Node Not Ready

patterns.match: kubelet has not checked in with the API server

possible_causes: Kubelet process has crashed or been OOMKilled on the node
Node is experiencing hardware or hypervisor failure
Network partition isolating the node from the control plane
Node disk pressure or memory pressure triggering NotReady condition
containerd or Docker runtime on the node has crashed
Cloud provider instance failure or unexpected maintenance event
recommended_fixes: Drain and cordon the node: kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets
SSH to the node and restart kubelet: systemctl restart kubelet
Inspect node resource usage: kubectl describe node <node>
Replace the failing node in managed node groups via cloud console
Check containerd/Docker daemon: systemctl status containerd
Increase cluster autoscaler budget if node pool is under-capacity

title: Kubernetes ResourceQuota Exceeded
possible_causes: Namespace resource quota limits were set too low for current workload
Sudden scale-out increased pod count beyond quota.pods limit
Accumulated stale or failed pods not cleaned up consuming quota
CPU or memory requests in pod specs too high relative to quota
Multiple teams sharing a namespace competing for the same quota
HPA scaled replicas beyond quota before limits were adjusted
recommended_fixes: Increase the namespace quota: kubectl edit resourcequota <name> -n <namespace>
Clean up failed or completed pods to release quota
Reduce CPU/memory requests in pod specs to lower quota consumption
Split workloads into separate namespaces with dedicated quotas
Review and set LimitRange to cap per-pod resource requests
Align HPA maxReplicas with namespace quota headroom

title: Pod Evicted — Node Pressure

patterns.match: The node was low on resource

possible_causes: Node disk usage crossed the kubelet eviction threshold
Node memory pressure caused the kubelet to evict lower-priority pods
PodDisruptionBudget constraints caused involuntary disruption
Cluster autoscaler preempted the pod for a higher-priority workload
Ephemeral storage limit in the pod spec was exceeded
Node running too many pods, causing memory or disk saturation
recommended_fixes: Free disk space on the node: clean images, logs, tmp files
Increase node memory or disk resources in your node pool
Set resource requests and limits to allow proper scheduling
Use PodPriorityClass to protect critical workloads from eviction
Enable cluster autoscaler to prevent node saturation
Configure ephemeral-storage limits in the pod spec

title: Liveness Probe Failure

patterns.match: restarting container due to liveness probe failure

possible_causes: Application entered a deadlocked or non-responsive state
Liveness probe endpoint is too slow relative to the timeout setting
Application is overloaded and cannot respond to health checks in time
Liveness probe path was changed in code but not in the pod spec
Insufficient initialDelaySeconds causing probe to run before app is ready
Out-of-memory condition causing health-check handler to fail
recommended_fixes: Increase initialDelaySeconds to give the application more startup time
Tune timeoutSeconds and failureThreshold to allow transient slowness
Verify probe path/port match the running application configuration
Switch to a lightweight health-check endpoint not blocked by app logic
Investigate thread deadlocks or GC pause times in application logs
Add more memory headroom to prevent OOM conditions affecting health checks

title: Readiness Probe Failure — Pod Removed from Service
possible_causes: Application startup is still in progress (dependency connections pending)
Underlying service dependency (database, cache) is not reachable
Readiness probe endpoint returns non-2xx during initialization
Application failed to start but container is still running
Incorrect readiness probe path, port, or scheme configured
Transient overload causing the app to temporarily fail health checks
recommended_fixes: Increase initialDelaySeconds on the readiness probe
Verify the readiness probe path matches the actual healthcheck route
Fix the underlying dependency (database, cache) causing the probe to fail
Check if the app is actually running: kubectl exec <pod> -- ps aux
Review endpoint registration: kubectl describe endpoints <service>
Separate readiness probe (dependency check) from liveness probe (alive check)

title: Database Deadlock Detected

patterns.match: deadlock found when trying to get lock
could not serialize access due to concurrent update
detected deadlock while waiting for lock
transaction rolled back due to deadlock

possible_causes: Multiple transactions acquiring locks in inconsistent ordering
Long-running transactions holding locks under high concurrency
Missing indexes causing full-table locks on UPDATE or DELETE
Application using SELECT FOR UPDATE without proper transaction scoping
Bulk data migrations running concurrently with production traffic
Inefficient ORM-generated queries holding row locks unnecessarily
verification_steps: EXPLAIN ANALYZE <slow-query> to identify missing indexes
recommended_fixes: Enforce a consistent lock acquisition order across all transactions
Add database indexes to reduce full-table scans under concurrent load
Set a statement_timeout to abort long-running blocking transactions
Use optimistic locking or MVCC instead of SELECT FOR UPDATE where possible
Reduce transaction scope to hold locks for the shortest possible duration
Monitor pg_locks / InnoDB lock waits and alert on threshold exceeded

title: Database Slow Query / Statement Timeout

patterns.match: canceling statement due to statement timeout

possible_causes: Missing database indexes causing full table scans on large tables
Unbounded query result set fetching millions of rows without pagination
Outdated table statistics causing the query planner to pick bad plans
N+1 query pattern generated by ORM without eager loading
Database under memory pressure causing disk-based sort or hash operations
Inefficient join ordering or missing composite index
verification_steps: EXPLAIN ANALYZE <query> to inspect the execution plan
SELECT * FROM pg_stat_statements ORDER BY mean_exec_time DESC LIMIT 10
recommended_fixes: Add the missing index identified by EXPLAIN ANALYZE
Run ANALYZE on affected tables to update planner statistics
Add LIMIT or pagination to queries returning unbounded result sets
Enable slow query log: log_min_duration_statement = 1000 (ms)
Refactor ORM queries to use eager loading (JOIN FETCH / includes)
Partition large tables to reduce per-query scan ranges

title: Database Migration / Schema Change Failure
possible_causes: Migration script contains a SQL syntax error or targets a nonexistent column
Migration running against wrong schema version due to out-of-order deploy
Insufficient database permissions to run DDL (ALTER TABLE, CREATE INDEX)
Migration holding a long lock while production traffic is active
Partial migration left schema in inconsistent state after a previous failure
Incompatible migration between two concurrently deployed application versions
verification_steps: SELECT * FROM schema_migrations ORDER BY version DESC LIMIT 20
SELECT * FROM flyway_schema_history ORDER BY installed_on DESC LIMIT 10
recommended_fixes: Review migration SQL for syntax errors and validate against current schema
Run migration in a dry-run / preview mode before applying to production
Ensure the database user has DDL privileges: GRANT ALL ON SCHEMA public
Use a migration repair command: flyway repair or alembic stamp head
Apply backward-compatible migrations (expand/contract pattern)
Run migrations in a maintenance window to avoid production lock contention

title: Database Replication Lag
possible_causes: Replica is being overwhelmed by write volume from the primary
Network bandwidth between primary and replica is saturated
Replica disk I/O is slower than the write-ahead log generation rate
Long-running query on the replica blocking replication apply
Replication slot accumulating unreplicated WAL segments filling disk
Primary experienced a high-load spike that replica is catching up from
verification_steps: SELECT now() - pg_last_xact_replay_timestamp() AS replication_lag ON REPLICA
kubectl exec <replica-pod> -- df -h to check disk space for WAL
recommended_fixes: Reduce write load on primary or add read replicas to distribute reads
Increase replica instance size (CPU, disk IOPS, network bandwidth)
Terminate long-running queries on the replica blocking apply
Monitor and alert on replication lag with pg_stat_replication
Drop unused replication slots that are accumulating WAL segments
Use synchronous_commit = off to reduce replication overhead if tolerable

title: Redis Out of Memory

patterns.match: ERR command not allowed when used memory
out of memory, consider using eviction

possible_causes: Redis maxmemory limit set too low for current data volume
TTL not set on keys causing unbounded memory growth
Eviction policy set to noeviction preventing new writes when full
Large Lua scripts or sorted sets consuming unexpected memory
Cache warming after restart loading more data than configured capacity
Memory fragmentation ratio high after many small object deletions
verification_steps: redis-cli DEBUG SLEEP 0 to check responsiveness
redis-cli MEMORY DOCTOR for Redis memory diagnostics
recommended_fixes: Increase maxmemory: redis-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory <new-value>
Set a sensible eviction policy: CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
Audit keys without TTL: redis-cli --scan | xargs redis-cli TTL | grep -c '\-1'
Set TTL on all cacheable keys to prevent unbounded accumulation
Scale Redis instance to a larger memory tier
Enable Redis memory defragmentation: CONFIG SET activedefrag yes

title: Redis Connection Timeout / Failure

patterns.match: could not get redis connection from pool

possible_causes: Redis pod crashed or was OOMKilled
Network policy blocking traffic to the Redis port (6379)
Redis connection pool exhausted due to connection leaks
Redis is overwhelmed and not responding to commands within timeout
Redis service endpoint not yet available after a restart
Wrong Redis host or port configured in application environment
recommended_fixes: Verify Redis pod is running: kubectl get pods -l app=redis
Check Redis logs for OOM or crash: kubectl logs <redis-pod>
Increase connection pool size and add connection retry logic
Verify REDIS_URL environment variable is correctly set
Check network policies allowing port 6379
Restart Redis if it is in a degraded state: kubectl rollout restart

title: Redis Replication Broken

patterns.match: Error condition on socket for SYNC

possible_causes: Network interruption between Redis primary and replica
Redis primary ran out of memory and became unresponsive
Replication buffer overflow caused replica to be disconnected
Clock skew between nodes causing replication state confusion
Redis version mismatch between primary and replica
Large RDB snapshot generation blocking the primary during sync
recommended_fixes: Check primary Redis health and restart if needed
Increase client-output-buffer-limit for replication: CONFIG SET client-output-buffer-limit
Trigger manual resync: redis-cli REPLICAOF NO ONE then REPLICAOF <master>
Verify network connectivity between primary and replica pods
Increase repl-backlog-size to tolerate temporary network interruptions
Upgrade both primary and replica to the same Redis version

title: Message Consumer Lag
possible_causes: Message broker is down or experiencing high load
Consumer processing is too slow relative to the message production rate
Consumer group rebalance triggered by node failure or deployment
Topic partition count too low for the current message throughput
Poison-pill message causing consumer to stall and retry indefinitely
Consumer application OOMKilled or crashing mid-batch
verification_steps: Check broker health: kubectl get pods -l app=kafka
Monitor topic metrics via broker admin API
recommended_fixes: Increase consumer parallelism by adding more consumer replicas
Increase topic partition count to allow higher consumer parallelism
Identify and skip or quarantine the poison-pill message
Investigate consumer application for processing bottlenecks
Use HPA to auto-scale consumers based on consumer lag metric
Tune consumer batch size and poll interval for throughput

title: Message Broker Unavailable

patterns.match: could not connect to any broker

possible_causes: Message broker pod(s) have crashed or been OOMKilled
Broker disk is full, preventing new message writes
Broker quorum lost due to multiple node failures simultaneously
Network partition isolating broker nodes from producers and consumers
Broker under-provisioned for the current message throughput
Rolling update of broker nodes disrupting in-flight replication
recommended_fixes: Restart failed broker pods: kubectl rollout restart statefulset/<broker>
Check disk space on broker nodes: kubectl exec <pod> -- df -h
Verify broker JVM heap settings match container memory limits
Check and resolve network policies blocking broker port 9092
Scale broker replicas to restore quorum after node failure
Review replication factor to ensure fault tolerance

title: Message Queue / Topic Full
possible_causes: Consumer lag has grown so large that the queue storage limit is reached
Retention policy (time or bytes) is too small for the message production rate
Consumer is down, allowing messages to accumulate without being processed
Unexpected message production spike without corresponding consumer scaling
Broker disk filled with log segments before retention cleanup could run
Topic partition count insufficient, causing a small number of partitions to overflow
verification_steps: Review broker retention settings: log.retention.bytes, log.retention.hours
recommended_fixes: Scale consumer replicas to start draining the queue immediately
Increase log.retention.bytes or log.retention.hours for the topic
Add more disk capacity to broker nodes
Increase topic partition count to distribute load
Implement producer back-pressure handling to slow production
Set consumer.max.poll.records higher to drain faster per poll

title: Cloud IAM / Authorization Denied

patterns.match: not authorized to perform this action

possible_causes: IAM role or policy missing required action permission for the resource
Workload identity or pod service account not bound to the correct IAM role
IAM assume-role trust policy does not include the calling service
SCP (Service Control Policy) at the organization level blocking the action
Resource-based policy (e.g., S3 bucket policy) denying cross-account access
Credential rotation invalidated the active session token mid-request
verification_steps: kubectl get iamserviceaccount -n <namespace> (if using AWS IRSA)
Check cloud provider audit logs for the exact denied API call
Review IAM role trust relationships and permission boundaries
recommended_fixes: Add the required permission to the IAM role: aws iam attach-role-policy
Bind the pod service account to the correct IAM role via IRSA or Workload Identity
Review and update resource-based policies on the target resource
Check for SCP denials at the organization or account level
Rotate credentials and re-attach the correct IAM policy
Use the principle of least privilege and audit permissions quarterly

title: Object Storage Access Failure
possible_causes: IAM/service account lacks the required read/write permission on the bucket
Bucket or container name is incorrect or does not exist
Object storage endpoint is unreachable due to a network policy
Presigned URL or SAS token has expired
Cross-region replication delay causing objects to appear missing
VPC endpoint for object storage not configured, causing traffic to route externally
verification_steps: aws s3 ls s3://<bucket>/ or gsutil ls gs://<bucket>/
Check pod service account annotations for IAM role binding
aws s3api head-bucket --bucket <bucket> to verify bucket existence
Review cloud audit logs for the specific denied API call
recommended_fixes: Grant the required IAM permissions (s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject) to the pod role
Verify the bucket name and region in application configuration
Configure a VPC endpoint for object storage to ensure private connectivity
Refresh expired presigned URLs or SAS tokens
Update bucket policy to allow cross-account access if needed
Enable server-side logging on the bucket to capture all access attempts

title: Cloud API Rate Limit Exceeded
possible_causes: Application is making too many API calls without exponential back-off
Multiple replicas all hitting the same API endpoint simultaneously
Polling loop with too short an interval hitting a rate-limited API
Burst traffic spike causing sudden spike in downstream API calls
Insufficient API quota allocated for the workload tier
Missing caching layer causing repeated identical API calls
verification_steps: Check cloud API metrics dashboard for request rate trends
Review application polling or API call intervals in configuration
Check API quota limits in cloud console
Correlate spikes in 429 errors with deployment events or traffic patterns
recommended_fixes: Implement exponential back-off with jitter for all external API calls
Add a caching layer (Redis, in-memory) to reduce redundant API calls
Increase API quota tier through cloud provider support
Reduce polling frequency or switch to event-driven / webhook pattern
Distribute API calls across multiple accounts or credentials if permitted
Use circuit breaker to stop hammering a throttled API automatically

title: Infrastructure Capacity Exceeded
possible_causes: Cloud availability zone does not have the requested instance type available
Node pool maximum size is too small for the current autoscaler demand
Cluster autoscaler cannot scale due to incorrect IAM permissions
Reserved instance limits reached in the cloud account
Hard quota on the number of vCPUs or instances in the cloud account
All nodes in the pool are at maximum pod density (maxPods limit)
verification_steps: Check cloud console for node pool scaling activity
recommended_fixes: Increase the maximum size of the node pool in the cloud console
Add a second node pool with a different instance type or in a different AZ
Request quota increase for the required instance type in the cloud account
Use Spot or preemptible instances to access spare capacity more easily
Enable cluster autoscaler with expander=priority to prefer available AZs
Review pod resource requests to ensure they are accurate and not over-requested

title: SSL/TLS Certificate Expired

patterns.match: certificate has expired or is not yet valid

possible_causes: Certificate renewal automation (cert-manager) failed or was not configured
Let's Encrypt ACME challenge blocked by network policy
Certificate secret was manually deleted or overwritten
Clock skew on the node makes a valid certificate appear expired
Self-signed certificate issued with too short a validity period
Wildcard certificate expired and was not propagated to all services
verification_steps: kubectl get secret <tls-secret> -o jsonpath='{.data.tls\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -dates
recommended_fixes: Renew certificate immediately: kubectl annotate certificate <name> cert-manager.io/issue-once=true
Install and configure cert-manager for automatic renewal
Verify ACME challenge is reachable: check network policies and ingress rules
Sync the renewed certificate secret to all namespaces using kubernetes-replicator
Add certificate expiry monitoring alerts (e.g., Prometheus ssl_certificate_expiry_seconds)
Set certificate renewalBefore to 30 days before expiry as a buffer

title: TLS Handshake Failure

patterns.match: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority
handshake: failed to verify server certificate
SSL peer certificate or SSH remote key was not OK

possible_causes: Server TLS certificate is expired or self-signed without a trusted CA
Certificate Authority (CA) chain is incomplete or missing intermediate certs
TLS version mismatch between client and server (e.g., TLS 1.1 disabled)
Mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate is expired or missing
DNS resolution pointing to wrong IP causing certificate CN/SAN mismatch
Custom CA certificate not injected into application trust store
verification_steps: kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt | openssl crl2pkcs7 -nocrl | openssl pkcs7 -print_certs -noout
recommended_fixes: Renew the expired certificate and update the Kubernetes TLS secret
Add the custom CA certificate to the pod trust store via ConfigMap mount
Ensure the full certificate chain (including intermediates) is in the secret
Align TLS version settings: set ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3
Verify DNS hostname matches the certificate CN or SAN fields
Use cert-manager to manage and auto-renew certificates

title: Network / Connection Timeout
possible_causes: Target service pod is overloaded and not responding in time
Network policy or firewall rule dropping packets between pods
Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) timeout configuration is too aggressive
VPC routing misconfiguration causing packets to be black-holed
Target service has crashed and no new connections are being accepted
Database or cache is under heavy load causing slow response times
recommended_fixes: Increase timeout values in the service mesh or application config
Scale up the target service to handle the request rate
Review and fix network policies blocking the traffic path
Add a circuit breaker to fail fast instead of waiting for timeout
Check for pod anti-affinity causing all pods to land on a congested node
Use distributed tracing (Jaeger/Zipkin) to identify the slow component

title: HTTP 5xx Gateway Error
possible_causes: All upstream service pods are down or not ready
Circuit breaker is open, blocking traffic to the upstream
Ingress controller cannot reach the backend service
Service mesh sidecar has failed, preventing pod-to-pod communication
Upstream pod is crashing (CrashLoopBackOff) and cannot accept connections
Load balancer target group has no healthy instances
recommended_fixes: Scale up or restart the upstream service pods
Investigate why upstream pods are not ready: kubectl describe pod <pod>
Check endpoints are populated: kubectl get endpoints <service>
Verify ingress rules and backend service selectors match pod labels
Check circuit breaker status and reset if conditions are resolved
Enable pod disruption budget to ensure minimum availability during updates

title: Service Circuit Breaker Open
possible_causes: Downstream service failure rate exceeded the circuit breaker threshold
Downstream service is experiencing high latency beyond the configured timeout
Network timeout storm causing consecutive failures to trip the breaker
Circuit breaker failure threshold tuned too aggressively for normal traffic variance
Downstream dependency was taken offline for maintenance without coordinating callers
Resource exhaustion (thread pool, connection pool) causing all calls to fail
verification_steps: Check Hystrix dashboard or resilience4j metrics endpoint
Review downstream service health: kubectl get pods -l <downstream-selector>
recommended_fixes: Fix the root cause in the downstream service (restart, scale, or patch)
Wait for the circuit breaker half-open retry period to automatically reset
Manually reset the circuit breaker if the downstream service is healthy
Tune circuitBreaker.failureRateThreshold and slowCallRateThreshold
Implement a fallback response to degrade gracefully during open state
Add retry logic with exponential back-off before hitting the circuit breaker

title: Load Balancer Health Check Failure
possible_causes: Application pods are not responding to health check requests
Health check path or port configured incorrectly in the load balancer
Application startup is slow and health checks fail before readiness
Security group or network ACL blocking health check traffic from the LB
All target instances crashed simultaneously, leaving no healthy targets
Stale target registration — terminated instances still listed as targets
verification_steps: Check load balancer access logs for health check request patterns
Verify security groups allow inbound traffic from LB subnet
recommended_fixes: Verify the health check path and port match the application
Fix the application pod so it responds with 200 on the health path
Update security group rules to permit LB health check traffic
Increase health check timeout and interval to tolerate slow startup
Deregister stale instances from the target group
Scale up healthy pods and allow the LB to re-add them after passing checks
